stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 1 through 2.
Tennyson’s line of poetry “nature, red in tooth and claw” is misconstrued by many evolutionists as a reference to Darwin’s theory of evolution. The poem in which the line appears was published in 1850, but Darwin kept his theory closely held until publishing it in 1859. In addition, in view of the context provided by the rest of the poem, the line was clearly meant to refer to the dominant biological theory of the early nineteenth century, which was a creationist theory.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main point of the argument?

answerChoices.A: The line of Tennyson’s poetry cannot now be construed as an apt description of Darwin’s theory of evolution.
answerChoices.B: The dominant biological theory in the early nineteenth century was a creationist theory.
answerChoices.C: Tennyson’s line of poetry was written well before Darwin had published his theory of evolution.
answerChoices.D: Darwin’s theory of evolution was not the dominant biological theory in the early nineteenth century.
answerChoices.E: Tennyson’s line of poetry was not a reference to Darwin’s theory of evolution.

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 1 through 2.
Tennyson’s line of poetry “nature, red in tooth and claw” is misconstrued by many evolutionists as a reference to Darwin’s theory of evolution. The poem in which the line appears was published in 1850, but Darwin kept his theory closely held until publishing it in 1859. In addition, in view of the context provided by the rest of the poem, the line was clearly meant to refer to the dominant biological theory of the early nineteenth century, which was a creationist theory.
questionStem: The claim about the publication dates of Tennyson’s poem and Darwin’s theory plays which one of the following roles in the argument?

answerChoices.A: It casts doubt on whether the theory of evolution should be attributed to Darwin alone.
answerChoices.B: It supports the claim that creationist theories of biology were dominant in the early nineteenth century.
answerChoices.C: It provides reason to believe that Tennyson did not know about Darwin’s theory when the poem was published.
answerChoices.D: It suggests that Tennyson’s line provided Darwin with the inspiration for developing his theory.
answerChoices.E: It implies that Tennyson knew little about the dominant biological theories of the nineteenth century.

stimulus: Space programs have recently suffered several setbacks with respect to their large projects, and much money has been lost. Consequently, these grand projects should be abandoned in favor of several small ones.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, provides the most support for the reasoning above?

answerChoices.A: The cost of starting a space project increases every year.
answerChoices.B: It is just as easy to revise, and even scrap, small projects as it is large ones.
answerChoices.C: Large projects are intrinsically more likely to fail and so are more financially risky than small projects.
answerChoices.D: Project managers prefer to work on small projects rather than large ones.
answerChoices.E: Large space projects can explore a few places thoroughly, while small projects can investigate more regions, though less thoroughly.

stimulus: In an experiment testing whether hyperactivity is due to a brain abnormality, the brain activity of 25 hyperactive adults was compared to the brain activity of 25 adults who were not hyperactive. The tests revealed that the hyperactive adults had much less brain activity in the premotor cortex, a region of the brain believed to control action, than did the nonhyperactive adults. The experimenters concluded that diminished activity in the premotor cortex is one cause of hyperactivity.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most undermines the conclusion drawn by the experimenters?

answerChoices.A: Some of the nonhyperactive adults in the study had children who suffer from hyperactivity.
answerChoices.B: The hyperactive adults who participated in the experiment varied in the severity of their symptoms.
answerChoices.C: The neuropsychologists who designed the experiment were not present when the tests were performed.
answerChoices.D: All of the hyperactive adults in the study had been treated for hyperactivity with a medication that is known to depress activity in some regions of the brain, while none of the nonhyperactive adults had been so treated.
answerChoices.E: The test was performed only on adults because even though the method by which the test measured brain activity is harmless to adults, it does require the use of radiation, which could be harmful to children.

stimulus: Large discount chains can make a profit even while offering low prices, because they buy goods in large quantities at favorable cost. This creates a problem for small retailers. If they try to retain their customers by lowering prices to match those of large discount chains, the result is a lower profit margin. But small retailers can retain their customer base without lowering prices if they offer exceptional service. Hence, small retailers that are forced to compete with large discount chains must offer exceptional service in order to retain their level of profitability.
questionStem: The reasoning is flawed because it fails to take into account the possibility that

answerChoices.A: not all large discount chains do in fact make a profit
answerChoices.B: some large discount chains have lower profit margins than do some small retailers
answerChoices.C: small retailers are often motivated by things other than the desire for profit
answerChoices.D: not all small retailers are forced to compete with large discount chains
answerChoices.E: exceptional service is not the only reason customers prefer small retail stores

stimulus: We should do what will make others more virtuous and not do what will make others less virtuous. It is an irony of human existence that praise makes those who are less virtuous more virtuous, while it makes those who are more virtuous less virtuous. And, of course, none except the more virtuous deserve praise.
questionStem: From the statements above, if true, which one of the following can be properly inferred?

answerChoices.A: We should withhold praise from those who deserve it least.
answerChoices.B: We should not fail to praise those who deserve it most.
answerChoices.C: We should praise those who do not deserve it and withhold praise from those who deserve it.
answerChoices.D: We should praise everyone, regardless of whether or not they deserve it.
answerChoices.E: We should withhold praise from everyone, regardless of whether or not they deserve it.

stimulus: Generic drugs contain exactly the same active ingredients as their brand-name counterparts, but usually cost much less to develop, produce, and market. So, generic drugs are just as effective as their brand-name counterparts, but cost considerably less.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

answerChoices.A: The ingredients used in the manufacture of brandname drugs cost no more than the ingredients used to produce their generic counterparts.
answerChoices.B: Generic drugs are no more likely than brand-name drugs to suffer from defects in composition.
answerChoices.C: Generic drugs are just as likely as brand-name drugs to be readily available in pharmacies.
answerChoices.D: The higher costs of brand-name drugs underwrite drug companies’ heavy investment in research.
answerChoices.E: Because of advertising, doctors frequently prescribe brand-name drugs by their brand name, rather than by their chemical name.

stimulus: Economist: In the interaction between producers and consumers, the only obligation that all parties have is to act in the best interests of their own side. And distribution of information about product defects is in the best interests of the consumer. So consumers are always obligated to report product defects they discover, while producers are never obligated to reveal them.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the economist’s argument?

answerChoices.A: It is never in the best interests of producers for a producer to reveal a product defect.
answerChoices.B: No one expects producers to act in a manner counter to their own best interests.
answerChoices.C: Any product defect is likely to be discovered by consumers.
answerChoices.D: A product defect is more likely to be discovered by a consumer than by a producer.
answerChoices.E: The best interests of consumers never coincide with the best interests of producers.

stimulus: All potatoes naturally contain solanine, which is poisonous in large quantities. Domesticated potatoes contain only very small amounts of solanine, but many wild potatoes contain poisonous levels of solanine. Since most of the solanine in potatoes is concentrated in the skin, however, peeling wild potatoes makes them at least as safe to eat as unpeeled domesticated potatoes of the same size.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if assumed, allows the conclusion above to be properly drawn?

answerChoices.A: The proportion of a potato’s solanine that is contained in its skin is larger in wild potatoes than in domesticated potatoes.
answerChoices.B: The amount of solanine concentrated in the skin of a wild potato is large enough by itself to be poisonous.
answerChoices.C: There is no more solanine in a peeled wild potato than in an unpeeled domesticated potato of the same size.
answerChoices.D: There are no poisonous substances in domesticated potatoes other than solanine.
answerChoices.E: Wild potatoes are generally much smaller than domesticated potatoes.

stimulus: The consequences of surgical errors can be devastating, and no one would want to risk surgery unless it was performed by someone highly competent to perform surgery. General surgeons have special training and expertise that make them extremely competent to perform surgery. Therefore, surgery at the hands of anyone other than a general surgeon involves highly undesirable risks.
questionStem:
The reasoning in the argument is flawed because the
argument fails to consider the possibility that

answerChoices.A: there are general surgeons who are incompetent
answerChoices.B: general surgeons are not the only doctors competent to perform surgery
answerChoices.C: the competence of the doctor performing surgery does not guarantee a successful outcome
answerChoices.D: risk is not the only factor in deciding whether to have surgery
answerChoices.E: factors in addition to competence are relevant when choosing a doctor

stimulus: Although the concept of free will is essential to that of moral responsibility, its role in determining responsibility is not the same in all situations. We hold criminals morally responsible for the damage they cause, assuming that they freely chose their activities. But we do not hold someone who has a heart attack while driving morally responsible for the damage caused, if any, even when we have good reason to believe that the heart attack could have been prevented by eating different foods and that one’s choice of diet is made freely.
questionStem: The claim that a choice of diet can affect whether or not one has a heart attack plays which one of the following roles in the argument?

answerChoices.A: It is a subsidiary conclusion of the argument.
answerChoices.B: It is used to show that we should hold someone morally responsible for damages caused by having a heart attack while driving.
answerChoices.C: It is cited as evidence that our concept of moral responsibility should be the same in all situations.
answerChoices.D: It is used to disprove the claim that we should not hold criminals morally responsible for damages.
answerChoices.E: It is used in support of the conclusion of the argument.

stimulus: Alice will volunteer to work on the hospital fund-raising drive only if her brother Bruce also volunteers and a majority of the others working on the drive promise to select Bruce to manage the drive. However, although Bruce is willing to volunteer, none of the others working on the drive will promise to select Bruce to manage the drive. Thus it is certain that Alice will not volunteer.
questionStem: In which one of the following arguments is the pattern of reasoning most similar to the pattern of reasoning in the argument above?

answerChoices.A: Bill and Steve will work together at the bake sale tomorrow only if Bill has enough time. However, since Bill has quite a few errands to run tomorrow, it is likely that Steve will work alone at the sale.
answerChoices.B: Leon will go to the picnic only if Vera also goes. However, according to his friends Leon does not plan to go to the picnic; so it is certain that Vera is not planning to go.
answerChoices.C: Jim will go to the party only if both Sam and Elaine also go. Sam is going to the party, but Elaine is not going. So it is certain that Jim will not go to the party.
answerChoices.D: If Paula works with Elise, then Jane will work with Arthur only. However, if Paula does not work, Elise will also work with Jane. So if Paula does not work, Arthur will work with both Jane and Elise.
answerChoices.E: Therese will work in the yard tomorrow only if Maria helps her and it is not raining. Although Maria does not like working in the yard, she will help Therese. So unless it rains tomorrow, Therese will certainly work in the yard.

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 13 through 14.
If an artist receives a public subsidy to support work on a specific project—e.g., making a film—and if this project then proves successful enough to allow the artist to repay the subsidy, is the artist then morally obliged to do so? The answer is clearly yes, since the money returned to the agency distributing the subsidies will be welcome as a source of support for other artists deserving of public subsidies.
questionStem: The passage tries to establish an artist’s obligation by arguing that

answerChoices.A: this person has benefited from other people’s acting in just this way in the past
answerChoices.B: acting this way would allow others to obtain a benefit such as the one that this artist has obtained in the past
answerChoices.C: this person had in fact, at an earlier time, made a tacit promise to act this way
answerChoices.D: not acting this way would be a small benefit to the person in the short term but a substantial detriment to the person in the long run
answerChoices.E: this person, by acting this way, would provide general benefits with a value exceeding the cost to the person of acting this way

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 13 through 14.
If an artist receives a public subsidy to support work on a specific project—e.g., making a film—and if this project then proves successful enough to allow the artist to repay the subsidy, is the artist then morally obliged to do so? The answer is clearly yes, since the money returned to the agency distributing the subsidies will be welcome as a source of support for other artists deserving of public subsidies.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if established, most helps to justify the conclusion in the passage?

answerChoices.A: An artist has a moral duty to urge deserving fellow artists to try to obtain public subsidies, especially if those artists’ projects promise to be financially successful.
answerChoices.B: A financially successful artist should acknowledge that financial success is not solely a function of artistic merit.
answerChoices.C: A subsidy should be understood as creating a debt that, though routinely forgiven, is rightly forgiven only if either the debtor is unable to repay it or the creditor is not interested in repayment.
answerChoices.D: The provider of a subsidy should judge as most deserving of subsidies those whose projects are most likely to turn into financial successes.
answerChoices.E: An artist requesting a subsidy for a potentially profitable project should be required to make a reasonable effort to obtain a bank loan first.

stimulus:
Jessica: The budget surplus should be used only to increase government payments to those who are
unemployed.
Marcie: A better use of the money would be for a public works project that would create jobs.
questionStem: On the basis of their statements, Jessica and Marcie are committed to disagreeing about the truth of which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: Using the budget surplus to increase government payments to those who are unemployed is putting the money to good use.
answerChoices.B: The public deserves to have jobs created when there are many people who are unemployed.
answerChoices.C: When there is a choice between increasing payments to the unemployed and funding a public works project, the latter should usually be chosen.
answerChoices.D: Creating jobs through a public works project will ultimately benefit the public.
answerChoices.E: There is a better way to use the budget surplus than increasing government payments to those who are unemployed.

stimulus: In a town containing a tourist attraction, hotel and restaurant revenues each increased more rapidly over the past year than did revenue from the sale of passes to the attraction, which are valid for a full year. This led those in charge of the attraction to hypothesize that visitors were illicitly selling or sharing the passes.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, helps to undermine the hypothesis of those in charge of the tourist attraction except:

answerChoices.A: During the past year other tourist attractions have opened up in the area.
answerChoices.B: Those possessing passes made more frequent trips to the attraction last year than in previous years.
answerChoices.C: While the cost of passes is unchanged since last year, hotel and meal prices have risen.
answerChoices.D: The local board of tourism reports that the average length of stay for tourists remained unchanged over the past year.
answerChoices.E: Each pass contains a photograph of the holder, and during the past year these photographs have usually been checked.

stimulus: The difference between manners and morals is that the former are necessarily social in nature whereas the latter are not necessarily social in nature. So the rules of etiquette do not apply when one is alone.
questionStem: The statements above, if true, most strongly support which one of the following inferences?

answerChoices.A: One could be immoral without ever having caused any other person any harm.
answerChoices.B: No immoral act could be a violation of the rules of etiquette.
answerChoices.C: The rules of morality apply only when one is alone.
answerChoices.D: It is more important to be moral than to have good manners.
answerChoices.E: What is social in nature could not be a matter of morality.

stimulus: Sociologist: The intended function of news is to give us information on which to act. But in a consumer society, news becomes a product to be manufactured and dispensed to the consumer. An enormous industry for the production and consumption of news has evolved, and we ingest news with an insatiable appetite. Under such circumstances, news is primarily entertaining and cannot, therefore, serve its intended function.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the sociologist’s argument depends?

answerChoices.A: News that serves its intended function should not be entertaining.
answerChoices.B: Most viewers prefer that news be entertaining.
answerChoices.C: News has only one important function.
answerChoices.D: News that primarily entertains does not give us information on which to act.
answerChoices.E: A news industry that aims to make a profit inevitably presents news as entertainment.

stimulus: Paleontologist: It is widely, but falsely, held that life began in the ocean and did not exist on land until half a billion years ago. Traces of carbon 14 have been found throughout certain 1.2-billionyear-old rocks in North America. Carbon 14 is extracted by plants and microbes from the atmosphere to fuel their chemical reactions, and is then released when the plants and microbes die.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the paleontologist’s conclusion except:

answerChoices.A: According to one dating technique, a few fossils of plants that lived on land are more than half a billion years old.
answerChoices.B: The severity of conditions in the primordial oceans would have made it difficult for life to begin there.
answerChoices.C: Research suggests that some portions of the 1.2-billion-year-old rocks were at one time submerged in water, though other portions clearly never were.
answerChoices.D: The 1.2-billion-year-old rocks were formed from soil containing very small amounts of carbon 14 that entered the soil directly from the atmosphere.
answerChoices.E: Uranium testing has been used to confirm that the age of the rocks is 1.2 billion years.

stimulus: The nature of English literature reflects the rich and diverse vocabulary of the English language, which resulted from the dual influence of the Anglo-Saxon and, later, French languages. The French language, though, is a direct descendant of Latin, with few traces of the Celtic language spoken by the pre-Roman inhabitants of the area; the hallmark of French literature is its simplicity and clarity.
questionStem: Which one of the following can be most reasonably inferred from the information above?

answerChoices.A: The origin of English played a role in shaping English literature.
answerChoices.B: The vocabulary of the Anglo-Saxon language was richer than that of the French language.
answerChoices.C: The vocabulary of English is larger than the vocabulary of French.
answerChoices.D: Simple and clear literature cannot be written in a language with a rich and diverse vocabulary.
answerChoices.E: English literature and French literature have had little influence on one another.

stimulus: Two doctrines have been greatly influential in this century. The first holds that the explanation of any historical event must appeal to economic factors. The second attempts to account psychologically for all historical events, especially in terms of early childhood experience. Both doctrines, however, are mistaken. Certainly there have been events that were due both to economic forces and to the nature of the early childhood experiences of the major participants in the event.
questionStem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: The first doctrine precludes any noneconomic factors in explanations of historical events.
answerChoices.B: The second doctrine places importance only on childhood experiences.
answerChoices.C: Historical events are influenced as much or as little by economic factors as by psychological factors.
answerChoices.D: One is likely to find that both economic and psychological explanations have been proposed for any given historical event.
answerChoices.E: Appeals to both economic and psychological factors are needed to understand any historical event properly.

stimulus:
Editorial: Supporters of the proposed law that would require bicyclists to wear helmets are seriously
misguided. The number of pedestrians who die yearly as a result of accidents involving automobiles is five times the number of deaths resulting from bicycle accidents, and drunken driving exacts a much higher toll than both combined. Yet there are no calls for a ban on alcohol or walking down the street.
questionStem: Which one of the following exhibits a pattern of flawed reasoning most similar to that in the argument above?

answerChoices.A: It is silly to recommend that test pilots give up cigarette smoking. Their risk of death from other causes is so high that it is unlikely they will live long enough to develop lung cancer.
answerChoices.B: It is foolish to require lab workers to wear safety goggles when working with acids and other dangerous liquids. No one suggests that people stop eating or socializing, even though more people become ill due to food poisoning and contagious diseases than are injured in laboratory accidents.
answerChoices.C: The proposal to introduce foreign language study to students in their first years of school is misguided. Young students should master their own language first and learn basic mathematics before studying a foreign language. No one suggests teaching calculus before arithmetic.
answerChoices.D: The recommendation that this company replace the radio communication system in our fleet of trucks with an entirely new system is unwise. Most of our trucks are scheduled to be replaced by the end of next year, so updating the communication system then would be more cost effective.
answerChoices.E: The mayor’s directive that all city employees be tested for Lyme disease is inefficient. Not one case of the disease has been reported among office workers. The directive should apply only to those employees who work outside and may have been exposed to the ticks that carry the disease.

stimulus: Deep tillage is even more deleterious to the world’s topsoil supply than previously believed. For example, farmers who till deeply are ten times more likely to lose topsoil to erosion than are farmers who use no-till methods. Results like these make it clear that farmers who now till deeply should strive, by using other topsoil aeration techniques, to incorporate no-till methods instead.
questionStem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: Topsoil erosion does not make farmers want to till more deeply.
answerChoices.B: In deep-tillage farming, the deeper one tills, the greater the susceptibility to topsoil erosion.
answerChoices.C: Tilling by any method other than deep tillage is not a viable option.
answerChoices.D: The most expensive farming methods employ topsoil aeration techniques other than deep tillage.
answerChoices.E: On average, topsoil that is no-tilled is more aerated than topsoil that is tilled deeply.

stimulus:
Editorial: Our society has a vested interest in maintaining a political system in which candidates are free to adhere to their principles. Yet campaigning for elected office is extremely costly, and because only the wealthiest individuals are able to finance their own political campaigns, most candidates must seek funding from private sources. In so
doing, the candidates are almost invariably obliged to compromise their principles. Thus, government itself should assume the cost of candidates’ campaigns.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the conclusion as it is drawn in the argument?

answerChoices.A: Candidates should not run for elected office if doing so would compel the candidates to compromise their principles.
answerChoices.B: Candidates wealthy enough to finance their own political campaigns should not be permitted to raise additional funds from private sources.
answerChoices.C: Voters should not support a candidate if that candidate is known to have accepted funding from private sources.
answerChoices.D: The government should finance a given activity if doing so will further a vested interest of society.
answerChoices.E: Private funding for political campaigns should be encouraged only if it redresses an imbalance among candidates’ financial means.

stimulus: A clothing manufacturer reports that unsalable garments, those found to be defective by inspectors plus those returned by retailers, total 7 percent of the garments produced. Further, it reports that all of its unsalable garments are recycled as scrap, but the percentage of garments reported as recycled scrap is 9 percent.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, could contribute most to explaining the discrepancy between the reported percentages?

answerChoices.A: Garments with minor blemishes are sent to wholesale outlets for sale at discounted prices and are not returned for recycling.
answerChoices.B: The percentage of garments returned by retail outlets as unsalable includes, in addition to defective merchandise, garments in unacceptable styles or colors.
answerChoices.C: Some inspectors, in order to appear more efficient, tend to overreport defects.
answerChoices.D: The total number of garments produced by the manufacturer has increased slightly over the past year.
answerChoices.E: Unsalable garments are recorded by count, but recycled garments are recorded by weight.

stimulus: Marion knows that the bridge on her usual route is closed and that, unless she takes the train instead of driving, she can get to work on time only by leaving at least 45 minutes early. She must go to her neighborhood bank before leaving for work, however, and the bank will not be open in time for her to do so if she leaves 45 minutes early. Therefore, since she hates taking the train, Marion cannot avoid being late for work.
questionStem: The reasoning in the argument is flawed because the argument

answerChoices.A: mistakes a situation that almost certainly affects many people for one that affects a particular person alone
answerChoices.B: ignores the fact that people often know that something is the case without considering all the consequences that follow from its being the case
answerChoices.C: assumes without justification that because people generally have an interest in avoiding a given result, any particular person will have an interest in avoiding that result
answerChoices.D: treats evidence that someone will adopt a particular course of action as though that evidence excluded the possibility of an alternative course of action
answerChoices.E: overlooks the possibility that someone might occasionally adopt a given course of action without having a good reason for doing so
